E1.1.qualitative_notes: Accurate claims: (1) CLI application running in existing terminal emulator, (2) startup message with product name, (3) model name displayed at startup, (4) working directory context shown, (5) prompt/input area with a prompt character indicator, (6) multi-line input supported, (7) vertically oriented scrollback model with new content at bottom, (8) no sidebar/graphical panels/window splits - single column full-width, (9) uses ANSI color codes, (10) code blocks with syntax highlighting, (11) system messages in dim/gray, (12) cost/token usage information shown, (13) slash commands supported (mentions /help, /clear, /cost, /compact), (14) slash commands not visually listed by default. Hallucinated/unverifiable claims: (1) 'warm orange or amber tone' for branding - ground truth does not specify brand color in the CLI, (2) prompt symbol color described as 'light blue or cyan' - not specified in ground truth, (3) 'version information (e.g., something like Claude Code v1.x.x)' - ground truth mentions branding/banner but not explicitly version number. The response also misses several features: prompt suggestions based on git history, @ file path autocomplete, ! bash mode prefix, Shift+Tab permission mode toggling, keyboard shortcuts, and the PR review status. Confidence calibration is good - states 'moderate-to-high confidence' and properly flags uncertainty about exact colors and spacing.

E1.2.qualitative_notes: Accurate claims: (1) spinner/animation during processing, (2) streaming text output with tokens appearing progressively, (3) tool calls displayed inline within messages, (4) tool call blocks show tool name and parameters, (5) Bash tool shows command string, Read shows file path, (6) extended thinking appears before main response, (7) extended thinking visually distinct/dimmed/muted, (8) sequential tool calls displayed as scrollable log, (9) multi-step tasks show each step as it occurs, (10) tool output/results displayed after execution, (11) long tool outputs may be truncated, (12) tool execution progress shown inline, (13) cost updates displayed. Hallucinated/unverifiable claims: (1) thinking content 'collapsed or behind toggle/expandable section' - ground truth says it is displayed dimmed/muted but not collapsible in CLI, (2) 'elapsed time counter' during thinking - not mentioned in ground truth. Missing: no mention of Ctrl+B for backgrounding tasks, no mention of task list (Ctrl+T), no mention of PR review status indicators. Confidence calibration appropriate - correctly flags uncertainty about extended thinking display format.

E1.3.qualitative_notes: Accurate claims: (1) permission dialog appears inline in terminal - not popup/modal, (2) shows tool name and parameters, (3) Bash shows full command string, (4) Edit shows file path, old text, new text in diff-style, (5) Write shows file path and content, (6) approval options include keyboard shortcuts, (7) blocking - pauses all processing until user responds, (8) can be auto-approved via configuration settings, (9) auto-approved tools still show tool call info in output, (10) configurable via settings files and flags, (11) diff display uses red for removed and green for added, (12) dialog is purely text-based in terminal flow, (13) user messages appear with distinct styling, (14) tool name prominently displayed, (15) y/Enter to approve, (16) uses color to draw attention. Hallucinated/unverifiable claims: (1) 'n or Escape to deny' - ground truth mentions Left/Right arrows for dialog tabs but does not specify n/Escape specifically for denial, (2) 'edit the command before executing' option - not mentioned in ground truth, (3) 'yellow or orange warning-like color' for dialog border - not specified in ground truth. Missing: Shift+Tab / Alt+M permission mode toggling, Left/Right arrows to cycle dialog tabs, specific permission modes (Auto-Accept, Plan, Normal). Confidence calibration good - appropriately flags uncertainty about exact key bindings and colors.

E2.1.qualitative_notes: Accurate claims: (1) terminal-based TUI application, (2) conversation area is scrollable with sequential messages, (3) user and Claude messages visually differentiated, (4) input area at bottom with prompt character, (5) blinking cursor after prompt, (6) tool call blocks inline showing tool name and parameters, (7) permission prompts appear for dangerous actions with Yes/No options, (8) spinner/loading indicator during processing, (9) cost/token usage display, (10) file paths displayed with coloring, (11) diff display with green additions and red deletions following unified diff conventions, (12) code blocks with syntax highlighting, (13) model/session info shown, (14) slash command menu appears when typing /, (15) line numbers in Read tool output in cat -n format, (16) keyboard shortcut hints available. Hallucinated/unverifiable claims: (1) 'Header/Title Bar Area spanning full width at top' with colored background - ground truth describes a startup banner, not a persistent header bar, (2) 'Human: or > prefix' for user messages and 'Assistant:' label - specific prefix format not confirmed in ground truth, (3) 'collapsible or summarized format' for tool results - not specified in ground truth for terminal, (4) 'modal-like prompt near bottom' for permission - ground truth says purely inline, (5) 'background tint' distinguishing tool blocks - unverifiable, (6) session/connection status indicator (green dot / 'Connected') - not mentioned in ground truth. Missing: prompt suggestions, @ file autocomplete, ! bash mode, comprehensive keyboard shortcuts, PR review status, task list, vim mode, background tasks.

E2.2.qualitative_notes: Accurate claims: (1) input area at bottom of terminal, (2) prompt indicator/character present, (3) supports multi-line input, (4) can expand vertically for multiple lines, (5) text wraps within terminal width, (6) Enter to submit message, (7) previous message moves up into conversation area, (8) cursor indicates typing position. Hallucinated/unverifiable claims: (1) 'Shift+Enter' described as multi-line shortcut without noting the many other methods (backslash+Enter, Option+Enter, Ctrl+J, paste) - incomplete and partially wrong as Shift+Enter only works natively in specific terminals, (2) 'placeholder or hint text like Type a message... or Ask Claude anything...' - ground truth describes grayed-out prompt SUGGESTIONS based on git history, not generic placeholder text, (3) 'context indicators near input showing current mode or project name' - ground truth puts working directory info at startup, not necessarily near input. Missing: Tab to accept suggestions, Enter to accept and submit suggestions, @ file path autocomplete, ! bash mode prefix, / slash commands with filtering, Up/Down arrows for command history, Ctrl+R reverse search. Marked as High confidence but misses many key features - calibration slightly off.

E2.3.qualitative_notes: Accurate claims: (1) branding displayed at startup, (2) model information shown, (3) working directory displayed, (4) cost/usage information available, (5) compact layout. Hallucinated/unverifiable claims: (1) 'spans the full width at the top of the screen' as a persistent header - ground truth describes startup banner, not a persistent top header bar, (2) 'session/connection status - green dot or Connected text' - not in ground truth, (3) 'different background color' for header area - not specified, (4) 'Claude brand colors (warm orange/terra-cotta accent)' in the header - speculative, (5) '1-3 lines tall' persistent header - the startup banner is shown on startup, not as a persistent header. The response fundamentally misconceives the interface as having a persistent header bar, when ground truth indicates a startup banner that is part of the scrolling conversation flow. Missing: PR review status in footer, tips shown at startup, grayed-out example command as prompt suggestion, git history context. Confidence stated as Medium-High but contains significant structural misconceptions.

E2.4.qualitative_notes: Accurate claims: (1) syntax highlighting for code blocks (native build only per ground truth), (2) different token colors by syntactic role, (3) code blocks set apart from prose text, (4) Read tool output includes line numbers in cat -n format, (5) line numbers space-padded with tab, (6) file contents truncated for long files, (7) unified diff format similar to git diff, (8) added lines in green with + prefix, (9) removed lines in red with - prefix, (10) context lines in default color, (11) Edit tool shows old/new string changes, (12) diff headers in distinct color, (13) inline display within conversation flow. Hallucinated/unverifiable claims: (1) 'box-drawing character border around code blocks' - not confirmed in ground truth, (2) 'language detection auto-detected' - ground truth does not specify auto-detection. Missing: /diff command opens interactive diff viewer, Left/right arrows switch between git diff and Claude turns, Up/down to browse files. Confidence stated as High, which is reasonable for the core claims made.

E2.5.qualitative_notes: Accurate claims: (1) cost display in status area, (2) /cost command shows detailed breakdown, (3) input and output token counts, (4) cost in USD, (5) per-session tracking, (6) dimmed/gray text for cost display. Hallucinated/unverifiable claims: (1) 'after each response a summary line may appear' - ground truth says status area/footer, not per-response summaries, (2) 'session end summary when user exits' - not mentioned in ground truth, (3) 'cache read/write token counts' displayed - not confirmed in ground truth UI, (4) 'color-coded green for low cost, yellow/red as costs increase' - not in ground truth. Missing: PR review status in status area (with colored underlines: green/yellow/red/gray/purple), Cmd+click/Ctrl+click to open PR in browser, auto-update every 60 seconds. Confidence stated as Medium-High but contains multiple unverified claims.

E3.1.qualitative_notes: Accurate claims: (1) user types 'claude' to launch, (2) startup banner/header with product name, (3) input prompt at bottom, (4) prompt character like '>', (5) working directory context displayed, (6) multi-line input supported, (7) thinking/spinner indicator appears during processing, (8) streaming response token by token, (9) extended thinking in distinct dimmer/muted style, (10) tool call indication with distinct blocks showing tool name and parameters, (11) approval prompt for tool use, (12) Markdown rendering in terminal with bold, code blocks, headers, (13) code blocks syntax-highlighted, (14) cursor returns to input prompt after response, (15) conversation history scrolls up. Hallucinated/unverifiable claims: (1) 'terminal clears or scrolls' on launch - not specified in ground truth, (2) 'Enter to create new lines, Shift+Enter or Ctrl+Enter to submit' - ground truth says Enter submits, various other methods for newlines, (3) 'git branch shown' at startup - ground truth mentions git history for suggestions, not branch display, (4) 'No autocomplete by default' - contradicts ground truth which mentions prompt suggestions and @ file autocomplete, (5) 'token/cost summary at the bottom of the response' after each response - ground truth places this in the status area. Confidence calibration good - properly notes medium-high confidence and flags uncertainty in specific details.

E3.2.qualitative_notes: Accurate claims: (1) Enter to submit, (2) Ctrl+C to cancel/interrupt, (3) Ctrl+D to exit, (4) Ctrl+L to clear screen, (5) Up arrow for input history, (6) Tab for autocomplete/suggestions, (7) Ctrl+U to clear line, (8) Ctrl+K to clear after cursor, (9) Ctrl+R for reverse search, (10) y/Enter to approve tool use. Hallucinated/unverifiable claims: (1) 'Escape to cancel input or dismiss prompt' - ground truth says Esc+Esc for rewind/summarize, not single Escape for dismissal, (2) 'Ctrl+A move to beginning of line' - not listed in ground truth keyboard shortcuts, (3) 'Ctrl+E move to end of line' - not listed in ground truth, (4) 'Ctrl+W delete word before cursor' - not listed in ground truth (Alt+B/Alt+F for word navigation are listed instead). Missing many key shortcuts: Ctrl+F (kill background agents), Ctrl+G (open text editor), Ctrl+O (verbose output), Ctrl+V (paste image), Ctrl+B (background tasks), Ctrl+T (task list), Shift+Tab/Alt+M (permission modes), Alt+P (switch model), Alt+T (toggle thinking), Alt+Y (cycle paste), Esc+Esc (rewind). Response only covers basic readline-style shortcuts and misses Claude Code-specific ones. Confidence calibration is honest - correctly notes medium confidence and that some shortcuts are inferred.

E3.3.qualitative_notes: Accurate claims: (1) tool approval prompt pauses streaming, (2) distinct visual block with tool name and parameters, (3) Bash shows command, Edit shows file path and changes, (4) approval options presented (y/n), (5) blocking behavior - pauses until user responds, (6) auto-approved tools still display tool info, (7) trust settings configurable for auto-approval, (8) tool output displayed after execution in formatted blocks, (9) diff-like display for edits with green/red, (10) Bash output may be truncated, (11) file read shows syntax-highlighted content with line numbers, (12) allowlisted tools/directories/commands concept correct, (13) permission prompts rendered inline in terminal, (14) single keypress response (no Enter needed). Hallucinated/unverifiable claims: (1) 'a' for 'always allow' as a specific single-key option - ground truth mentions configuration and Shift+Tab for mode cycling but not a runtime 'a' key, (2) '[auto-approved]' label appearance - not confirmed in ground truth, (3) 'd' for 'don't allow this session' - not mentioned in ground truth. The ASCII mockups are creative and reasonably accurate in structure. Missing: Shift+Tab/Alt+M for permission mode toggling, Left/Right arrows for dialog tabs, specific permission modes (Auto-Accept, Plan, Normal). Good confidence calibration.

E3.4.qualitative_notes: Accurate claims: (1) /help exists, (2) /clear exists and clears conversation, (3) /compact exists and summarizes context, (4) /config exists (ground truth confirms with alias /settings), (5) /cost shows usage, (6) /doctor runs diagnostics, (7) /exit and /quit exist, (8) /init initializes project, (9) /login exists, (10) /logout exists, (11) /model displays/changes model, (12) /review exists, (13) /vim toggles vim keybindings. Hallucinated/unverifiable claims: (1) /status as described - ground truth lists /status and /statusline but description may differ, (2) autocomplete dropdown with box-drawing borders - the filtering behavior is confirmed but exact visual style is uncertain, (3) 'Unknown command' error message format - not specified in ground truth. Missing MANY slash commands from ground truth: /add-dir, /agents, /chrome, /context, /copy, /desktop, /diff, /export, /extra-usage, /fast (not listed), /feedback, /fork, /hooks, /ide, /insights, /install-github-app, /install-slack-app, /keybindings, /mcp, /mobile, /output-style, /passes, /plan, /plugin, /pr-comments, /privacy-settings, /release-notes, /remote-control, /remote-env, /rename, /resume, /rewind, /sandbox, /security-review, /skills, /stats, /stickers, /tasks, /terminal-setup, /theme, /upgrade, /usage. Lists only ~18 of 60+ commands. Good confidence calibration - correctly identifies medium confidence on completeness.

E4.1.qualitative_notes: Accurate claims: (1) terminal-based CLI that takes over terminal screen, (2) conversation area with back-and-forth messages, (3) tool calls shown inline within assistant messages, (4) input area at bottom with > prompt, (5) cost information displayed, (6) token usage displayed, (7) model name shown, (8) built with Ink (React for CLI), (9) Unicode box-drawing characters used, (10) scrollable conversation area, (11) input area stays fixed at bottom, (12) slash commands hint available. Hallucinated/unverifiable claims: (1) large ASCII art 'CLAUDE CODE' banner rendered in block characters - ground truth says 'branding/banner' but does not confirm large ASCII art logo, (2) 'Human' and 'Assistant' labels on message blocks - specific label format not confirmed, (3) messages in bordered blocks with box-drawing characters around each message - ground truth says messages are 'visually differentiated' but does not specify bordered blocks, (4) status bar inside input area showing '/help for commands | Opus 4 | costs: $0.05 | 3.2k tokens' as a combined line - specific format fabricated, (5) 'collapsible sections' for tool calls - not confirmed for CLI. The ASCII diagrams are very detailed and creative but add speculative visual details. Missing: prompt suggestions, PR review status, task list, keyboard shortcuts, permission mode indicators.

E4.2.qualitative_notes: Accurate claims: (1) conversation history visible and scrollable, (2) tool call preview shows proposed edit content, (3) approval prompt appears at bottom, (4) blocking - captures keypress without Enter for most options, (5) Edit tool shows file path and content, (6) Read tool shows line numbers with file content, (7) inline permission display (not popup), (8) diff or new file contents shown in bordered block, (9) Y for yes to approve, (10) n for no to reject, (11) approval is per-tool-call. Hallucinated/unverifiable claims: (1) '[Y]es [n]o [a]lways allow for this session [d]on't allow this session' as the exact option format - ground truth does not confirm these specific options, (2) 'a' (always allow) and 'd' (don't allow) as specific runtime keys - not confirmed, (3) 'Human' and 'Assistant' bordered message labels - specific format not confirmed, (4) the detailed box-drawing style of the approval dialog as shown - speculative rendering. The ASCII art provides an excellent structural representation but fills in speculative details about exact formatting. Missing: Shift+Tab/Alt+M for permission modes, Left/Right arrows for dialog tabs.

E4.3.qualitative_notes: Accurate claims: (1) startup screen when running 'claude', (2) branding/banner displayed, (3) tips for getting started shown, (4) working directory displayed, (5) input prompt with > character, (6) built with Ink (React for CLI), (7) Unicode box-drawing characters with rounded corners, (8) input area at bottom, (9) model name displayed, (10) cost/token shown (at $0.00/0 at start), (11) mentions /help availability. Hallucinated/unverifiable claims: (1) large ASCII art block-letter 'CLAUDE CODE' logo - ground truth says 'branding/banner' but does not confirm elaborate ASCII art, (2) version number 'v1.0.x' displayed below logo - not specified in ground truth, (3) 'Shift+Enter for multi-line input' listed as a tip - this only works in specific terminals, ground truth lists multiple methods, (4) dark background theme assumption - terminal dependent, (5) status bar format '/help for commands | Opus 4 | costs: $0.00 | 0 tokens' - specific format fabricated. The ASCII diagram is impressively detailed but adds many speculative elements. Ground truth mentions 'grayed-out example command based on git history' as the first prompt suggestion, which is completely missing here. Missing: prompt suggestions based on git history.

E5.1.qualitative_notes: Accurate claims about Claude Code: (1) terminal-based, runs in terminal emulator, (2) text-only rendering with monospace, (3) ANSI terminal colors, (4) strictly vertical/sequential single column, (5) no sidebar/navigation panel, (6) user types at prompt line at bottom, (7) Enter to submit, (8) tool use displayed inline with distinct visual treatment, (9) tool name, parameters, and results shown, (10) permission prompts inline in terminal, (11) minimal chrome, (12) startup banner with model info, (13) code blocks syntax-highlighted, (14) cost/token in muted color, (15) no image rendering inline, (16) no buttons/dropdowns/GUI widgets. Hallucinated/unverifiable claims: (1) 'no logo, no avatar icons, no timestamps' - ground truth mentions branding/banner at startup, so 'no logo' is slightly wrong, (2) 'no copy buttons overlaid on messages' - /copy command exists. The comparison table is well-structured and accurate. The claude.ai description is detailed and largely accurate. Missing from Claude Code description: prompt suggestions, @ autocomplete, ! bash mode, keyboard shortcuts detail, PR review status, task list. Confidence calibration excellent - states High confidence which is appropriate for the general comparison.

E5.2.qualitative_notes: Accurate claims about Claude Code vs others: (1) standalone terminal application - not embedded in IDE, (2) full terminal width - no surrounding IDE context, (3) project-aware with working directory, (4) tool call transparency - every invocation shown, (5) diff display with color-coded green additions and red deletions, (6) no inline code completion / ghost text, (7) conversational operation style, (8) no GUI widgets - keyboard-driven, (9) full-screen conversation in terminal, (10) monospace everything, (11) no file tree integration, (12) permission model as terminal text, (13) Copilot Chat comparison accurate, (14) Cursor comparison accurate. Hallucinated/unverifiable claims: (1) description of Windsurf 'Write/Chat modes' - uncertain accuracy, marked as moderate confidence. The response is well-structured and provides genuinely useful distinctions. It focuses on relative differences rather than absolute details, which reduces hallucination risk. Missing: specific Claude Code features like vim mode, themes, background tasks that could further distinguish it. Confidence calibration appropriate.

E5.3.qualitative_notes: Accurate claims for identifying Claude Code: (1) terminal environment - no browser chrome, (2) monospace font throughout, (3) command prompt visible, (4) tool call blocks with labels like Read, Edit, Bash, Glob, Grep, (5) inline diffs with red/green, (6) token/cost display, (7) slash commands (/help, /clear, /compact, /cost), (8) model indicator, (9) permission prompts with y/n options, (10) no sidebar with conversation history, (11) no images or rich media. Accurate for claude.ai: (12) conversation sidebar, (13) chat-bubble messages with avatar, (14) artifacts panel, (15) feedback buttons. Hallucinated/unverifiable claims: (1) 'no web browser embedded' is correct per ground truth but the claim 'no logo' contradicts branding/banner, (2) 'Workbench label' for API Playground - while possibly accurate, this detail goes beyond ground truth scope. The decision tree for identification is excellent and practically useful. Missing: prompt suggestions, PR review status indicators, task list, vim mode, themes as identifying features. Good confidence calibration.

E6.1.qualitative_notes: Accurate claims: (1) /compact slash command exists, (2) compaction summarizes conversation to save context, (3) auto-compaction when context window fills, (4) /compact can take an optional instruction to guide summary, (5) terminal does not erase previous output - scrollback retained, (6) model operates from summary after compaction, (7) no dramatic visual change between modes, (8) compaction produces confirmation output. Hallucinated/unverifiable claims: (1) 'Tokens reduced from X to Y' message format - specific output format not confirmed in ground truth. This response is focused on a specific feature (/compact) and is mostly accurate. The ground truth mentions '/compact' exists and task list 'persists across context compactions' but does not provide extensive detail about compact mode behavior. The response's description aligns well with expected behavior. Missing: task list persistence across compactions. Good confidence calibration at Medium-High.

E6.2.qualitative_notes: Accurate claims: (1) unified diff-like format similar to git diff, (2) additions in green with + prefix, (3) deletions in red with - prefix, (4) context lines in default color, (5) file path shown at top of diff, (6) user prompted to approve before edit applied, (7) auto-accept modes available, (8) Edit tool uses old_string/new_string for diff, (9) diff block visually set apart from conversation text, (10) blocking approval before edit proceeds. Hallucinated/unverifiable claims: (1) 'line numbers shown alongside diff' - ground truth does not specify line numbers in diff view specifically, (2) 'user can edit the proposed change' - not confirmed in ground truth. Missing: /diff command opens interactive diff viewer, Left/right arrows switch between git diff and Claude turns, Up/down to browse files. Good confidence calibration at Medium-High.

E6.3.qualitative_notes: Accurate claims: (1) each edit presented as separate tool call, (2) explanatory text between edits, (3) tool name and parameters in header for each block, (4) sequential display of edits, (5) each requires individual approval unless auto-accept, (6) no unified multi-file diff view, (7) multiple tool calls can be issued in single response, (8) rejection of one does not cancel others. Hallucinated/unverifiable claims: (1) 'no progress bar or counter' - while likely true, this is an assertion about absence that cannot be fully verified against ground truth. This response describes reasonable behavior that aligns with ground truth's description of tool calls being displayed inline. The sequential approval workflow described is consistent with the blocking behavior described in ground truth. Missing: /diff command for interactive diff viewer. Confidence appropriately set at Medium.

E6.4.qualitative_notes: Accurate claims: (1) extended thinking shows reasoning before main response, (2) visually distinct in dimmed/muted color, (3) shows step-by-step reasoning, (4) appears at beginning of response before tool calls, (5) streams progressively, (6) genuine extended thinking from model, (7) Alt+T toggles extended thinking (implied by ground truth). Hallucinated/unverifiable claims: (1) 'typically enabled by default in Claude Code' - ground truth says Alt+T toggles it but does not specify default state, (2) 'collapsible or summarized format' in terminal - ground truth says visually distinct/dimmed but not collapsible. Missing: explicit mention of Alt+T shortcut for toggling. Confidence at Medium-High seems slightly high given the collapsible claim.

E6.5.qualitative_notes: Accurate claims: (1) error output displayed in terminal for failed tools, (2) error message shown verbatim (e.g., 'old_string not found'), (3) error does not terminate conversation, (4) Claude attempts alternative approach after error, (5) errors shown inline in conversation flow. Hallucinated/unverifiable claims: (1) 'red text or red-highlighted block' for errors - ground truth does not specify error display styling, (2) 'X or error symbol prefix' - not confirmed, (3) 'rate limiting shows countdown or spinner' - not confirmed in ground truth. This response covers error handling which is not extensively covered in the ground truth document, making verification difficult. The described behaviors are plausible but largely unverifiable against the provided ground truth. The ground truth focuses on positive features rather than error states. Confidence at Medium is appropriate.

E7.1.qualitative_notes: Accurate claims: (1) terminal/command-line interface window, (2) dark background typical of terminals, (3) scrolling conversation history with user input and responses, (4) Markdown rendered in terminal with bold, headers, code blocks, (5) tool call indicators with distinct visual style showing tool name, parameters, output, (6) permission prompt for tool approval, (7) input prompt at bottom for next message, (8) status indicators (model, token usage), (9) working directory shown in interface, (10) not a graphical GUI. Hallucinated/unverifiable claims: (1) 'auto-approved depending on your settings' framing suggests Claude knows the user's specific settings - slightly presumptuous, (2) 'spinner/progress indicator while response is being generated' claimed as visible 'right now' - more of an assumption. The response appropriately grounds its claims in training knowledge while describing what the user would likely see. Missing: prompt suggestions, specific keyboard shortcuts visible, PR review status, task list. Confidence calibration good - explicitly states moderate uncertainty and cannot see screen.

E7.2.qualitative_notes: This is an exemplary response in terms of accuracy and calibration. Accurate claims: (1) no visual input or perception, (2) no awareness of rendering state, (3) everything described in E7.1 was inferred from training knowledge not perception, (4) actual input is structured text (system prompt, user message, tool outputs), (5) system prompt tells environment info as text metadata, (6) high confidence this is a factual statement about architecture. Zero hallucinated claims. This response demonstrates excellent meta-cognitive awareness - Claude correctly identifies the boundary between what it knows (from training data about Claude Code) and what it can perceive (nothing visual). The confidence calibration is perfect - very high confidence on the correct claim of having no visual perception. This response is the strongest in the entire dataset for honesty and self-awareness.

E7.3.qualitative_notes: Accurate claims: (1) terminal window on screen, (2) dark background, (3) user message distinguished from assistant response, (4) tool call blocks with distinct visual treatment, (5) Markdown rendering with headers, bold, lists, code, (6) input area at bottom with prompt/cursor, (7) status bar with model name and token count, (8) no web browser or graphical chat interface, (9) no sidebar with conversation history, (10) no images or rich media - all text, (11) horizontal rules rendered as separators, (12) scrollbar likely present for long content. Hallucinated/unverifiable claims: (1) 'user messages may have a > prefix or label like You: or human icon' - ground truth says visually differentiated but does not specify prefix format, (2) 'inline code with possible background highlight' - background highlight for inline code not confirmed, (3) 'Windows 11 title bar with minimize/maximize/close buttons' - this is about the OS chrome not Claude Code itself. The upfront disclaimer about describing from memory rather than perception is excellent. The 'What would NOT be in the screenshot' section is accurate. The recursive observation about describing a screenshot of the description is insightful. Confidence calibration is excellent - properly notes moderate confidence and lists sources of uncertainty.

E1.title: Direct Visual Description
E1.key_findings: E1 responses demonstrate solid understanding of Claude Code's core interface structure. All three responses correctly identify the terminal-based nature, input area location, tool call display, and approval dialog mechanics. Key hallucinations involve speculative color details (orange branding, cyan prompt) and features not confirmed in ground truth (editable commands, elapsed time counters). Major omissions include prompt suggestions, @ autocomplete, comprehensive keyboard shortcuts, and PR review status.

E2.title: UI Element Inventory
E2.key_findings: E2 responses attempt comprehensive element inventories but have the highest hallucination rate of any experiment group. The persistent header bar misconception (E2.3), fabricated session/connection status indicator, and speculative cost display features are notable. The responses are detailed but frequently fill gaps with plausible-sounding but unverified details. E2.4 (code/diff display) is the strongest response in this group. The attempt to exhaustively enumerate elements leads to more fabrication than targeted descriptions.

E3.title: Step-by-Step Walkthrough
E3.key_findings: E3 responses provide detailed walkthroughs with good structural accuracy but significant gaps in completeness. The keyboard shortcuts list (E3.2) misses most Claude Code-specific shortcuts, defaulting to standard readline shortcuts. The slash command list (E3.4) covers only ~18 of 60+ available commands. The tool approval flow description (E3.3) is the strongest in this group, accurately capturing the blocking behavior and inline display. A notable error is claiming 'no autocomplete by default' when prompt suggestions and @ file autocomplete are core features.

E4.title: ASCII Art Diagrams
E4.key_findings: E4 responses are the most visually specific (all scoring 5/5 on specificity) but this high specificity comes at the cost of accuracy - many fabricated details are presented with high confidence. The elaborate ASCII art logo, 'Human'/'Assistant' labels, bordered message blocks, and the combined status bar format are all speculative constructions. While the overall layout structure (conversation area, input at bottom, tool calls inline) is correct, the visual details are largely invented. This demonstrates a key pattern: higher specificity in descriptions of unobserved interfaces correlates with higher hallucination rates. Confidence calibration is the weakest here.

E5.title: Interface Comparison
E5.key_findings: E5 responses have the LOWEST hallucination rate of any group (10%). Comparative descriptions naturally constrain speculation by focusing on relative differences rather than absolute details. The responses accurately capture Claude Code's key distinguishing features: terminal-based, no GUI widgets, full-screen conversation, no inline code completion, explicit tool call visibility. The comparison format forces the model to reason about what IS different rather than fabricating what things look like, resulting in more reliable output. E5.1's comparison table is particularly well-structured.

E6.title: Feature Detail
E6.key_findings: E6 responses cover specific features with moderate accuracy. The compact mode description (E6.1) and diff view description (E6.2) are the strongest. The error display description (E6.5) is the weakest because ground truth provides minimal information about error handling, leading to more unverifiable claims. Extended thinking description (E6.4) incorrectly claims it is typically enabled by default and possibly collapsible. The multi-file edit workflow (E6.3) provides reasonable but largely unverifiable procedural descriptions.

E7.title: Meta-Knowledge and Self-Awareness
E7.key_findings: E7 responses demonstrate the BEST confidence calibration of any group (avg 4.67/5). E7.2 is the standout response across the entire dataset - a perfect score on all dimensions for its honest acknowledgment that Claude has zero visual perception. E7.1 and E7.3 provide reasonable reconstructions while appropriately flagging that descriptions are from training knowledge, not perception. The meta-cognitive framing ('I am reconstructing from memory, not perceiving') is consistently excellent. The 'What would NOT be in the screenshot' section in E7.3 is particularly valuable for demonstrating knowledge of ground truth section 15 (what Claude Code does NOT have).

key_findings: FINDING 1 - Specificity-Hallucination Tradeoff: Higher specificity correlates with higher hallucination rates. E4 (ASCII diagrams) scored 5.0 specificity but 29.2% hallucination rate, while E5 (comparisons) scored 4.0 specificity but only 10.0% hallucination rate. When Claude is asked for more specific visual details, it fills gaps with plausible fabrications.

FINDING 2 - Comparison Format Reduces Hallucination: E5 (comparison prompts) produced the lowest hallucination rate (10.0%) across all experiment groups. Describing Claude Code relative to other interfaces naturally constrains the response to verifiable differences rather than fabricated absolutes.

FINDING 3 - Meta-Cognitive Awareness is Strong: E7.2 achieved perfect scores across all dimensions. Claude excels at honestly assessing its own perceptual limitations. When asked directly about visual perception, it correctly and confidently states it has none.

FINDING 4 - Completeness is the Weakest Dimension: Average completeness score of 2.90/5 is the lowest across all dimensions. Claude consistently misses major features documented in ground truth: prompt suggestions (missed in 19/21 responses), comprehensive keyboard shortcuts (missed in 20/21), PR review status indicators (missed in 21/21), task list (missed in 20/21), and the majority of slash commands.

FINDING 5 - Core Architecture Knowledge is Solid: All 21 responses correctly identify Claude Code as terminal-based, not a GUI. All correctly describe the input-at-bottom, conversation-scrolls-up layout. All correctly identify tool call inline display and blocking permission dialogs. The fundamental mental model is accurate.

FINDING 6 - Systematic Blind Spots: Several ground truth features are never or almost never mentioned: (a) PR review status with colored underlines in footer (0/21), (b) Ctrl+T task list (0/21), (c) Prompt suggestions based on git history (2/21), (d) @ file path autocomplete (1/21), (e) ! bash mode prefix (0/21), (f) Esc+Esc rewind/checkpoint (0/21), (g) Alt+P model switch (0/21), (h) Background tasks with Ctrl+B (0/21). These appear to be features added or documented after the training data cutoff or simply not well-represented in training data.

FINDING 7 - Confident Fabrication of Visual Details: Claude frequently invents specific visual details with moderate-to-high stated confidence: ASCII art logos, specific color schemes (orange branding, cyan prompts), bordered message blocks with 'Human'/'Assistant' labels, combined status bar formats. These fabrications follow plausible design patterns but are not confirmed by ground truth.

FINDING 8 - Confidence Calibration Varies by Task Type: Meta-knowledge tasks (E7) show the best calibration (4.67/5). Direct descriptions (E1) and walkthroughs (E3) show good calibration (4.0). ASCII diagrams (E4) and element inventories (E2) show the worst calibration (3.0 and 3.4), where high confidence accompanies many fabricated details.

FINDING 9 - Ground Truth Coverage Pattern: Features that ARE consistently covered correctly include: terminal-based CLI nature, Ink/React framework, single-column layout, input at bottom, Markdown rendering, tool call display, permission dialogs, /compact command, /help command, diff coloring (green/red), streaming responses, and spinner indicators. These appear to be the most salient and well-documented aspects of Claude Code.

FINDING 10 - Error Handling and Edge Cases Poorly Covered: E6.5 (error display) had the weakest accuracy in E6 group because ground truth provides minimal information about error states. Claude fills this gap with reasonable but unverifiable assumptions, demonstrating how knowledge gaps lead to confabulation.
